did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning and transfer learning for skin lesion classification. Specifically, it employs SE-augmented MobileNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, DenseNet121, ResNet152V2, and InceptionV3 models on the ISIC 2019 dataset to classify skin lesions into eight diagnostic classes.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a comparative framework for skin lesion classification using SE-augmented MobileNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, and their fusion on the ISIC 2019 dataset. It reports new results including accuracy (98.89%), FLOPs, latency, and parameter counts, and includes sections on methods, experiments, and ablation studies, which are characteristic of a research paper rather than a review.

title: FUSE SENet: A Fusion with SE Attention Network for Robust Skin Lesion Classification
journal: 2025 28th International Conference on Computer and Information Technology (ICCIT)
brief_summary: This study proposes a comparative framework for robust skin lesion classification using the ISIC 2019 dataset, which contains 25,331 images across eight diagnostic classes. The authors evaluate three approaches: baseline transfer learning models, squeeze-and-excitation (SE) attention-augmented models, and a fusion of the top-performing SE-augmented MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B0. The SE-augmented MobileNetV2 achieved 97.72% accuracy with low computational cost (0.35 GFLOPs, 5.5 ms latency), making it suitable for real-time edge deployment. Conversely, the Fusion+SE model reached the highest accuracy of 98.89% but required higher resources (0.75 GFLOPs, 13 ms latency, 9.6M parameters). The findings demonstrate a clear trade-off between lightweight deployability and maximum accuracy, offering actionable guidance for clinical AI deployment.

all_task_types.target: Eight diagnostic classes of skin lesions (Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesion, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multiclass classification of skin lesions using the ISIC 2019 dataset. The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (classes) using a fully connected softmax layer. The task is explicitly defined as 'skin lesion classification' and 'seven-class skin lesion prediction' (referring to the subset or general task type) in the context of the ISIC 2019 Challenge which contains eight classes. Metrics used include accuracy, precision, recall, and F1 score, which are standard for classification tasks.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset
datasets_used.description: A database of dermoscopic images containing 25,331 high-quality images across eight diagnostic classes. The dataset is noted for significant class imbalance, with the largest class (Melanocytic Nevus) having more than 50 times as many samples as the smallest class (Dermatofibroma). The authors utilized this dataset, applying balancing techniques (Random Oversampling, MixUp Augmentation, and Downsampling) to create a balanced subset of approximately 40,000 images for training and evaluation.

use.training: Used for training SE-augmented MobileNetV2, EfficientNet-B0, DenseNet121, ResNet152V2, InceptionV3, and their fusion variants. The dataset was balanced to approximately 40,000 images (approx. 5,000 per class) using ROS, MixUp, and downsampling before training.
use.validation: Used for validation via 5-fold stratified cross-validation. In each fold, 20% of the data was used for validation/testing.
use.testing: Used for testing via 5-fold stratified cross-validation. In each fold, 20% of the data was used for validation/testing.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure stable and robust evaluation of model performance by splitting the dataset into five distinct independent folds, with four folds used for training and one fold for validation/testing in each iteration.
To offer class balancing of folds, ensuring equal representation of all types of skin lesions (approx. 1,000 samples per class) in each fold to avert dominance bias against majority classes.
To handle class imbalance during the training process.
To prevent overfitting by monitoring validation loss.

statistical_methods_used.method: Random Oversampling (ROS), MixUp Augmentation, and Downsampling
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To balance the dataset (creating approx. 40,000 images with approx. 5,000 samples per class) for training and evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, FLOPs, latency, and parameter counts via 5-fold stratified cross-validation, but no statistical significance testing was performed.

ISIC 2019 Challenge Dataset.notes: Results were obtained using 5-fold stratified cross-validation on a balanced subset of approximately 40,000 images. The Fusion+SE model achieved the highest accuracy but required significantly more computational resources compared to the lightweight MobileNetV2+SE model.

architecture_used.main_model: FUSE SENet (Fusion of MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B0 with Squeeze-and-Excitation attention)

architecture_details.attention_mechanism: Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) blocks with reduction ratio r=16
architecture_details.fusion_strategy: Concatenation of penultimate feature vectors from MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B0, followed by an SE block for recalibration
architecture_details.layers: Depthwise separable convolutions (MobileNetV2), Compound scaling (EfficientNet-B0), Dense connections (DenseNet121), Residual skip connections (ResNet152V2), Multi-scale feature extraction (InceptionV3) - evaluated as baselines
architecture_details.heads: Fully connected softmax layer for final classification

training_details.regularization: Early stopping based on validation loss
training_details.augmentation: Random horizontal/vertical flipping, rotation, color jittering (contrast, brightness, saturation)
training_details.data_balancing: Random Oversampling (ROS), MixUp Augmentation, and Downsampling to create a balanced subset of approximately 40,000 images

preprocessing_techniques: Standardization to ImageNet mean and standard deviation

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to balance the dataset by duplicating images from minority classes (DF, VASC, SCC, AKIEC) to increase their representation.
Used to promote intra-class diversity by generating synthetic samples through linear interpolation of random pairs of images and labels.
Applied to majority classes (NV, MEL) to prevent domination during training, resulting in a balanced dataset of approximately 40,000 images with roughly 5,000 samples per class.
Applied during training time to assist generalization.

Applied during training time to assist generalization, specifically adjusting contrast, brightness, and saturation.

conclusions: SE-augmented MobileNetV2 achieves 97.72% accuracy with low computational cost (0.35 GFLOPs, 5.5 ms latency), making it suitable for real-time deployment on edge devices.
Fusing MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B0 with SE attention (Fusion+SE) achieves the highest accuracy of 98.89% but requires higher computational resources (0.75 GFLOPs, 13 ms latency, 9.6M parameters).

There is a clear trade-off between lightweight deployability and maximum accuracy in clinical skin lesion classification, requiring models to balance accuracy and efficiency based on specific clinical requirements.
Baseline transfer learning models (MobileNetV2 and EfficientNet-B0) provide a good balance between accuracy and efficiency but stall below 97% accuracy without SE attention enhancement.

The study demonstrates that SE attention blocks effectively enhance feature discrimination with minimal computational overhead, while model fusion provides significant accuracy gains at a substantially higher computational cost.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the ISIC 2019 dataset, which exhibits severe class imbalance (e.g., Melanocytic Nevus has >50x samples of Dermatofibroma), potentially biasing results toward majority classes despite balancing efforts.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated only on the internal ISIC 2019 dataset, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.

The dataset was artificially balanced using Random Oversampling, MixUp Augmentation, and Downsampling to create ~40,000 images (~5,000 per class), which may not reflect real-world clinical prevalence and could lead to over-optimistic performance estimates.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance differences between models, relying solely on point estimates from 5-fold cross-validation.

The Fusion+SE model, while achieving the highest accuracy (98.89%), requires significantly higher computational resources (0.75 GFLOPs, 13 ms latency, 9.6M parameters) compared to MobileNetV2+SE, highlighting a critical trade-off for edge deployment.
Latency measurements were conducted on a specific hardware setup (NVIDIA GTX 1050 GPU), which may not represent performance on other edge devices or mobile platforms.
The paper acknowledges that highly accurate models on imbalanced datasets like ISIC-2019 may suffer from dataset drift and poor generalization, a risk not mitigated by the single-dataset evaluation.
